title: Config register into async control — spurious setup violation
description:
# Spurious setup violation on config path

You're seeing a -420 ps setup violation from `cfg_reg/Q` into `mode_ctrl/D`. But the team lead tells you this path is *never* clocked dynamically — `cfg_reg` is only written during boot, and `mode_ctrl` samples it after a software-driven wait of thousands of cycles.

There's no combinational logic missing. The path is real, but it shouldn't be timed at 1 cycle.

Clock period is 0.8 ns. Uncertainty 50 ps. Investigate the constraint environment — the fix is in SDC, not in the netlist.

reportedViolation.summary: setup VIOLATED (-0.420 ns) on cfg_reg/Q -> mode_ctrl/D

rootCauseOptions.label: No timing exception defined: this is a quasi-static path that should carry a false_path or multicycle_path exception
decode_and and priority_mux are too slow and need resizing
The path has a missing buffer on n_cfg_q

hintTree.nudge: You can see the path is ~1.2 ns long but the clock is 0.8 ns. Before touching the netlist, ask yourself: should this path be timed single-cycle at all?
hintTree.hint: The lead said `cfg_reg` is written once at boot and read after thousands of cycles. Look at `report_exceptions` — what's currently constraining this path?
hintTree.walkthrough: Run `report_exceptions` — the output is empty. This path has no false_path or multicycle_path exception. Since `cfg_reg` is quasi-static, add `set_false_path -from cfg_reg/Q -to mode_ctrl/D`. If you want belt-and-suspenders safety, a multicycle_path with a generous N also works. No netlist changes needed.

steps.title: Read the timing report
steps.goal: Look at the failing path. How much delay is in the data path vs. the available clock period?

checkpoint.question: Roughly how many clock cycles does this data path need?

options.label: ~1.5 cycles (path is ~1.2 ns, period is 0.8 ns)
Less than 1 cycle — it should easily fit

steps.insight: The path is ~1.2 ns in a 0.8 ns clock. It can't possibly meet single-cycle timing. Before resizing anything, ask: does it *need* to be single-cycle?

steps.title: Understand the architectural context
steps.goal: Re-read the problem description. The team lead said something important about when cfg_reg is written and when mode_ctrl samples it.

checkpoint.question: Based on the architect's note, should this path be timed at 1 cycle?

options.label: No — cfg_reg is written at boot and mode_ctrl reads it thousands of cycles later
Yes — every reg-to-reg path must meet single-cycle by default

steps.insight: This is a quasi-static path — cfg_reg changes once at boot and never again during operation. The STA tool doesn't know this unless you tell it via an SDC exception.

steps.title: Check existing timing exceptions
steps.goal: See if someone already added a false_path or multicycle_path for this path.

checkpoint.question: Are there any timing exceptions defined?

options.label: No — the exception list is empty
Yes — there's already a false_path but it's wrong

steps.insight: Empty. This is the root cause — the tool is constraining a quasi-static path to single-cycle timing because nobody wrote the SDC exception.

steps.title: Choose the right SDC fix
steps.goal: Decide whether to use set_false_path or set_multicycle_path. Think about what's safer.

checkpoint.question: Which approach is more appropriate here?

options.label: set_false_path — the path is quasi-static and effectively never exercised dynamically
Resize decode_and and priority_mux to fit in one cycle

steps.insight: set_false_path is correct — this path doesn't need timing at all. A multicycle_path with a large N also works as a belt-and-suspenders approach. The key insight: not all violations need netlist changes. Some need SDC changes.
